next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

By nine the schoolyard smelled of old mud baked back into dust, goat dung, dried fish from somebody's breakfast basket, and the hot iron smell tin roofs made when the sun started climbing. Men had dragged the school benches out under the veranda because the two rooms inside could not hold the magistrate, the clerks, the disputed households, the watchers at the windows, and the bodies that had come simply because shame was worth seeing when it belonged to somebody else. Halima stood with the oilcloth ledger under her arm and felt the notebook's corners through the cloth, hard as the shoulder blades of starved children.

Mofiz had arrived early enough to arrange hospitality. A bamboo table stood under a white sheet. Three glasses of tea sweated rings onto a plastic tray. He had found an electric pedestal fan from somewhere on the eastern side and placed it so it turned first toward the magistrate's chair, then toward his own, then away from the crowd entirely. His beard edges were freshly dyed, too black against the gray in his cheeks, and every few minutes he patted the front of his panjabi as though the cloth itself might testify for him.

Aminul stood beside the table with his folders held off his chest so the papers would not drink his sweat. He had changed into a clean pale shirt and still looked tired enough for the cuffs to seem like punishment. Nura was a little behind him, papers squared in both hands, a pencil tucked over one ear. She had tied her hair tighter than usual. Whenever she was frightened she made herself look prepared enough to shame the wo...
open_hooks_to_carry: Sufia's betrayal is motivated by fear and misjudgment, not simple malice.
The copied pages connect Halima's merciful forgeries to Mofiz's opportunistic fraud.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he girl now. She overcontrolled when frightened. "Khodeja fupu's bedding has been sent to Khaleka's cousin's hut on the west side. Munni and Tuli are going to stay near the tea stall. Bashir's stepson has been told to sleep on the boat till after the hearing, because apparently a child on water doesn't count the same."

She handed the papers to Aminul and went back inside before either of them replied.

That was the hearing arriving early, not on a launch or under a magistrate's seal, but in rolled mats, moved plates, and children redirected for a few nights so the sentence would come out cleaner when spoken aloud.

Across the yard, a woman passed carrying an aluminum trunk on her head while an old man walked beside her with a cane and no pace left in him. Near the gate, a boy dragged a bedding roll twice his own width through the dust, leaving a stripe behind him like something erased. The river kept giving its distant soft collapses from the east, patient as paperwork.

Halima stood with chalk grit on her palm and understood at last that the coming hearing would not sort truth from falsehood in any way that mattered to the people living here. It would reward whichever version of care could be made to sound orderly without breaking in the witness box. She had spent half her life pulling extra bodies inside when weather, men, and money tried to shake them loose. Now the schoolroom had begun teaching the char the opposite lesson. Households rolled the mat, shifted the child, said she only came sometimes. By sunset several households had already learned it well enough to act.
state_deltas_required: Identify who weaponized the missing ledger pages and decide whether secrecy still offers any protection.
Halima learns Mofiz used copied pages to harden his own claims and decides to deploy the ledger publicly rather than keep defending it as a private moral reserve.
